sanskrit_devanagari:
अपरेयमितस्त्वन्यां प्रकृतिं विद्धि मे पराम् ।
जीवभूतां महाबाहो ययेदं धार्यते जगत् ॥ ५ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
apareyam itas tv anyāṁ prakṛtiṁ viddhi me parām |
jīva-bhūtāṁ mahā-bāho yayedaṁ dhāryate jagat || 5 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical).
exact (academic edition; text body identical; punctuation/danda rendering differs).
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_07.005 = MBh_06,029.005). Body text identical.

anvaya.meaning: the life-element / consisting of living beings

translations.verbatim_quote: Besides these, O mighty-armed Arjuna, there is another, superior energy of Mine, which comprises the living entities who are exploiting the resources of this material, inferior nature.

Such is My inferior energy. But beyond it, O mighty-armed Arjun, I have a superior energy. This is the jīva śhakti (the soul energy), which comprises the embodied souls who are the basis of life in this world.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Here it is clearly mentioned that living entities belong to the superior nature (or energy) of the Supreme Lord. The inferior energy is matter manifested in different elements, namely earth, water, fire, air.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.5 O mighty-armed one, iyam, this; is aparā, the inferior (Prakṛti) — not the higher, (but) — the impure, the source of evil and having the nature of worldly bondage. Viddhi, know; anyam, the other, pure; prakṛtim, Prakṛti; me, of Mine, which is essentially Myself; which is parām, higher.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads parā prakṛti as essentially identical with Krishna himself ('me, of Mine, which is essentially Myself') — the kṣetrajña, the knower-of-the-field. The aparā is the impure source of bondage; the parā is what holds the world up because it IS Krishna in jīva-form.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.5 This is My lower Prakṛti. But know My higher Prakṛti which is different from this, i.e., whose nature is different from this inanimate Prakṛti constituting the objects of enjoyment to animate beings. It is 'higher', i.e., is more pre-eminent compared to the lower Prakṛti.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads parā prakṛti as the animate enjoyer (jīva) as distinct from the inanimate enjoyed (matter). The two are different in nature; the higher is preeminent. Both are Krishna's prakṛti in the Vishishtadvaita sense (his śarīra), but they remain ontologically distinct from him.

positions.rendering: another superior energy comprising the living entities who exploit material nature
the jīva-śakti, the soul energy, basis of life in this world
essentially Krishna himself; the kṣetrajña; what holds the world up because it IS Krishna in jīva-form
the animate jīva as distinct from inanimate matter; both are Krishna's body but ontologically distinct from him

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The deepest doctrinal divergence in this verse. For Shankara, parā prakṛti is essentially Krishna himself (the knower-of-the-field) — the Advaita non-dualism is preserved. For Ramanuja, parā prakṛti is the jīva, which is Krishna's body (śarīra) but remains ontologically distinct from him (the qualified non-dualism). Prabhupada and Mukundananda follow the Vishishtadvaita reading. Engineering layers must be careful: the verse names a higher prakṛti distinct from the eightfold lower, but the metaphysical content of that distinction is contested across traditions.

literal_meaning: This (eightfold prakṛti) is the lower; but know my other prakṛti, the higher one, the life-element by which this world is sustained.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna distinguishes his two prakṛtis. The verse 7.4 named the lower (aparā) — the eightfold material substrate. Here he names the higher (parā) — the jīva-bhūta, the life-element, the principle by which the world is held up (dhāryate). Without the parā, the aparā does not 'hold' anything — there are no enjoyers, no living beings, no consciousness. The two-prakṛti structure is the chapter's foundational doctrinal move; verses 7.6-7.7 build on this distinction.
